description: UE5 data-driven design: DataTables, DataAssets, PrimaryDataAssets, CurveFloats, and the Asset Manager

entries.title: Data Tables
entries.description: Data Tables are spreadsheet-like assets that store rows of structured data. Each row is a struct. Designers can edit values in the editor without touching code. Essential for items, weapons, enemies, levels, dialogue, etc.
entries.whatItIs: A Data Table is a UE5 asset that stores an array of rows where each row is an instance of a user-defined USTRUCT derived from FTableRowBase. It acts like a spreadsheet keyed by row name, editable in-editor or importable from CSV/JSON.
whenToUse: Storing large collections of similar items with the same fields (weapons, items, enemies, dialogue lines)
When designers need to bulk-edit game data in a spreadsheet-like interface or import from external tools
When you want to look up a specific row by name at runtime (e.g., item ID to stats)
When importing balance data from external CSV or JSON files maintained by designers
whenNotToUse: Complex objects that have nested sub-objects, asset references, or polymorphic data — use Data Assets instead
When each entry is highly unique with different fields — Data Tables require all rows to share the same struct
When you need Blueprint-scriptable logic per entry — Data Table rows are plain structs, not UObjects
Very small datasets (1-3 entries) where individual Data Assets or config variables are simpler
commonMistakes: Forgetting to derive the row struct from FTableRowBase — the editor will not let you select it as a row type
Using hard object references (UStaticMesh*) in rows instead of soft references (TSoftObjectPtr), causing the entire table to load all referenced assets into memory
Not checking the return value of FindRow() for nullptr — the row name may not exist
Editing Data Tables at runtime and expecting changes to persist — runtime modifications are lost unless explicitly saved
Creating a new Data Table row struct for every table instead of reusing common base structs
keyPoints: Row struct: subclass FTableRowBase with UPROPERTY fields. This defines the columns.
Create DataTable: Content Browser > Miscellaneous > Data Table, select your row struct.
Edit in editor: double-click to open the table editor. Add/remove/edit rows.
Import from CSV/JSON: File > Import to create or update a DataTable from external data.
C++ lookup: DataTable->FindRow<FMyRow>(RowName, ContextString). Returns pointer to row or nullptr.
GetAllRows<FMyRow>(): get all rows as an array
FDataTableRowHandle: a UPROPERTY-friendly reference to a specific row in a specific table
Blueprint: use Get Data Table Row node with row name
Common use cases: item definitions, weapon stats, XP curves, dialogue lines, quest data

entries.title: Data Assets & Primary Data Assets
entries.description: Data Assets are UObject assets that store gameplay configuration. Unlike DataTables (spreadsheet), they are individual assets with full Blueprint/C++ support. PrimaryDataAssets add asset management capabilities.
entries.whatItIs: UDataAsset is a lightweight UObject-based asset for storing configuration data as individual .uasset files. UPrimaryDataAsset extends it with Asset Manager integration, giving each asset a unique PrimaryAssetId for async loading and bundle management.
whenToUse: Defining complex game entities with nested data, asset references, and polymorphic fields (character classes, ability definitions)
When each entry is unique enough to warrant its own asset file that can be independently versioned and referenced
When you need the Asset Manager to asynchronously discover, load, and unload data at runtime
When Blueprints need to directly reference a specific data definition as a UPROPERTY
whenNotToUse: Large collections of homogeneous items (100+ weapons with the same fields) — Data Tables are more efficient and easier to bulk-edit
When you just need a simple key-value config — a Data Table or project settings may be simpler
Temporary runtime data that does not need to be an asset on disk
When the data will be imported from CSV/Excel — Data Tables support this natively, Data Assets do not
commonMistakes: Using UDataAsset when UPrimaryDataAsset is needed — without PrimaryAssetId, the Asset Manager cannot discover or async-load the asset
Forgetting to override GetPrimaryAssetId() in PrimaryDataAsset subclasses, causing the asset to not register with the Asset Manager
Not configuring the Asset Manager scan paths in DefaultGame.ini for your PrimaryDataAsset types
Storing large runtime state in Data Assets — they are meant for static configuration, not mutable game state
Hard-referencing Data Assets everywhere instead of using soft references or the Asset Manager, defeating the purpose of async loading
keyPoints: UDataAsset: simple data container. Create subclass, add UPROPERTY fields.
Create instances: Content Browser > Miscellaneous > Data Asset, select your class.
Each instance is its own .uasset file — good for complex items with nested data.
UPrimaryDataAsset: extends UDataAsset with Asset Manager integration (async loading, bundles).
PrimaryAssetId: unique identifier (Type:Name) used by the Asset Manager for loading.
Override GetPrimaryAssetId() to define the asset type.
Asset Manager: scans for primary data assets and can load/unload them asynchronously.
Data Assets vs Data Tables: use tables for many similar items (100+ weapons), use assets for complex unique items (character classes, ability definitions)

entries.title: Curve Assets (Float, Vector, Color)
entries.description: Curve assets define values that change over time or over a parameter. Used for damage falloff, animation blending, difficulty scaling, color gradients, and more.
entries.whatItIs: Curve assets are UE5 assets that map a float input to a float, vector, or color output via editable keyframe curves. They let designers visually author non-linear relationships (e.g., distance to damage multiplier) without code changes.
whenToUse: Any gameplay value that varies non-linearly over a parameter — damage falloff by distance, difficulty scaling over time, speed over input
When designers need to visually tune a relationship curve without recompiling
Animation blending, camera shake intensity, audio volume curves
When you want to reuse the same curve across multiple systems via asset references
whenNotToUse: Simple linear interpolation between two values — just use FMath::Lerp instead of a full curve asset
Boolean or discrete state transitions — curves are for continuous values
When the mapping is a simple lookup table with exact values — a Data Table may be clearer
Extremely high-frequency sampling (thousands per frame) where curve evaluation overhead matters
commonMistakes: Forgetting to null-check the curve pointer before calling GetFloatValue() — crashes if the curve asset is not assigned
Using FRuntimeFloatCurve but never assigning it in the editor, resulting in a flat curve returning 0
Setting curve keys with incorrect tangent modes (auto vs linear vs constant) and getting unexpected interpolation
Creating separate curve assets when FRuntimeFloatCurve (inline editable) would be simpler and avoid extra asset management
Sampling a curve outside its defined key range without understanding the extrapolation behavior (clamp vs linear vs constant)
keyPoints: UCurveFloat: maps a float input to a float output via a curve. The most commonly used.
UCurveVector: maps float input to FVector output (3 curves for X, Y, Z).
UCurveLinearColor: maps float input to FLinearColor (4 curves for R, G, B, A).
Create: Content Browser > Miscellaneous > Curve > select type.
GetFloatValue(Time): sample the curve at a given time/parameter value.
Keyframes: add points in the curve editor. Control tangents for sharp or smooth curves.
External curves: use in Niagara, Materials, Timelines, and code.
FRuntimeFloatCurve: UPROPERTY-editable curve that can be inline (no separate asset) or reference an asset.
